title: Counterfactual Functional Connectomes for Neurological Classifier Selection
author: Vercheval, Nicolas
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0

steps.instruction: Functional connectivity expresses the correlation of brain activity between regions and helps understand and diagnose neurological conditions and disorders. It also provides discriminative features for machine learning classifiers. At the same time, no explanatory model exists for such classifiers to increase the trust in their actual application. The present contribution covers this gap. More than that, our approach strives for explainability and collaboration with medical experts, starting from the model selection. We propose a model-agnostic method that produces realistic counterfactual functional connectomes by altering the posterior distribution of a hierarchical variational auto-encoder. We introduce a denoising method that adequately counteracts the effect of posterior collapse, a well-known problem in variational auto-encoders, and restores the original sharpness of the input. We evaluate it on three autism spectrum disorder classifiers for resting state fMRI and present quantitative and qualitative results. We propose two metrics, in addition to the standard success percentage, to test the calibration of our method and the reliability with which the counterfactuals fool the classifiers. We show that it is consistently successful and well-calibrated. We illustrate its application on example test cases, forming a visual basis for comparing the selected classifiers. The generated counterfactuals include plausible changes in line with medical literature and the brain's functional anatomy.
